ABW: Aruba lies just south of the Caribbean hurricane belt, so direct hurricane hits are rare enough that locals can usually name the handful that happened from memory.
AFG: Afghanistan has changed its national flag more than 20 times since 1901, reportedly more than any other country in modern history — a reflection of just how many governments have risen and fallen there.
AGO: Despite being one of Africa's top oil producers, Angola still imports most of its refined gasoline because it has only one working refinery.
AIA: Anguilla has no rivers, lakes, or streams at all — every drop of fresh water comes from rain catchment or desalination.
ALA: Aland is Finnish territory where nearly everyone speaks Swedish, and it issues its own postage stamps and vehicle plates.
ALB: Albania built over 170,000 concrete bunkers under a Cold War-era regime for a population of under 3 million — more per capita than any other country.
AND: Andorra has one of the highest life expectancies in the world, credited partly to its mountain air and highly active population.
ARE: Dubai's police force patrols in a fleet that includes a Bugatti Veyron and a Lamborghini Aventador, marketed as the world's fastest police cars.
ARG: Buenos Aires' 9 de Julio Avenue is often called the widest avenue on Earth — wide enough to take several traffic-light cycles to cross on foot.
ARM: Armenia was the first country in the world to adopt Christianity as a state religion, in the year 301 AD.
ASM: American Samoans are U.S. nationals but not U.S. citizens by birth — a unique legal status shared by no U.S. state.
ATA: Antarctica is the only continent with no native population, no time zone of its own, and no country — it's governed instead by an international treaty.
ATF: The French Southern Territories have no permanent residents at all — only rotating scientists stationed on their remote subantarctic islands.
ATG: Antigua and Barbuda's tourism board famously claims '365 beaches, one for every day of the year' — a promotional line that's stuck for decades, even though the exact count depends on how you define a beach.
AUS: More than a third of Australia's landmass lies within the tropics, yet most Australians live along the temperate southeastern coast.
AUT: The Christmas carol 'Silent Night' was written in Austria and first performed in a small village church in 1818.
AZE: Azerbaijan's name is thought to mean 'land of fire,' inspired by natural gas seeps that have burned continuously from its hillsides for millennia.
BDI: Burundi is one of the least electrified countries on Earth — most of its population has never had access to grid electricity.
BEL: Belgium has three official languages — Dutch, French, and German — despite being smaller than the U.S. state of Maryland.
BEN: Benin is considered the birthplace of Vodun, which remains an officially recognized religion practiced by a large share of the population.
BFA: Burkina Faso's name means 'land of upright/honest people,' adopted after independence to replace its colonial name, Upper Volta.
BGD: Bangladesh is criss-crossed by hundreds of rivers and tributaries, giving it one of the densest river networks of any country on Earth — estimates of the total range from roughly 230 major rivers up to 700 once every tributary is counted.
BGR: Bulgarians traditionally nod their head for 'no' and shake it side to side for 'yes' — the reverse of most of the world.
BHR: Bahrain means 'two seas' in Arabic, referring to natural underground layers of fresh and salt water once found around the island.
BHS: The Bahamas' Exuma islands are home to a colony of wild pigs that swim out to greet passing boats, with no confirmed explanation for how they got there.
BIH: Sarajevo is nicknamed the 'Jerusalem of Europe' for its cluster of mosques, churches, and a synagogue all within a few blocks of each other.
BLM: Saint Barthelemy was a Swedish colony for nearly a century before France bought it back in 1878 — its capital, Gustavia, is still named for a Swedish king.
BLR: Nearly 40% of Belarus is covered in forest, home to some of the last European bison living in the wild.
BLZ: Belize is the only country in Central America where English is the official language, a legacy of British colonial rule as British Honduras.
BMU: Bermuda isn't in the Caribbean at all — it sits alone in the North Atlantic, over 1,000 km from the nearest Caribbean island.
BOL: Bolivia has two capitals: Sucre is the constitutional capital, while La Paz, the seat of government, sits 3,640 meters up — the highest administrative capital in the world.
BRA: Brazil shares a border with every South American country except Chile and Ecuador.
BRB: Barbados became the world's newest republic in 2021, removing the British monarch as head of state after nearly 400 years.
BRN: Brunei's sultan has a personal car collection reportedly numbering in the thousands, funded by vast oil and gas reserves.
BTN: Bhutan measures national progress with a 'Gross National Happiness' index instead of GDP, and is one of the only carbon-negative countries on Earth.
BWA: Botswana is home to more elephants than any other country on Earth, with a population estimated well over 100,000.
CAF: The Central African Republic sits almost exactly at the geographic center of the African continent.
CAN: Canada is home to roughly two million lakes — more than any other country on Earth, covering almost 9% of its total land area.
CHE: This country has enough nuclear fallout shelters to house more than 100% of its population.
CHL: Chile stretches over 4,300 km from north to south but averages only about 175 km wide, making it one of the longest, thinnest countries on Earth.
CHN: China spans five geographic time zones but uses only one official time zone nationwide.
CIV: Cote d'Ivoire's official capital is Yamoussoukro, but nearly every embassy and most government activity remains in the larger city of Abidjan.
CMR: Cameroon is nicknamed 'Africa in miniature' because it contains nearly every major climate and landscape on the continent, from desert to rainforest to snow-capped peaks.
COD: The Congo River is the deepest river in the world, plunging to depths of over 220 meters in places.
COG: Brazzaville and Kinshasa, capitals of the two Congos, sit on opposite banks of the same river — the closest two capital cities in the world.
COK: The Cook Islands are self-governing in free association with New Zealand, and residents hold New Zealand citizenship despite issuing their own currency and stamps.
COL: Colombia is the only South American country with coastlines on both the Pacific Ocean and the Caribbean Sea.
COM: The Comoro Islands are one of the world's largest producers of ylang-ylang, a flower essential to many high-end perfumes — the country is sometimes called the 'Perfume Islands' as a result.
CPV: Cape Verde was uninhabited until Portuguese sailors settled it in the 15th century, later making it a key hub in the transatlantic slave trade.
CRI: Costa Rica abolished its military entirely in 1948 and has remained one of the most stable democracies in Latin America ever since.
CUB: Cuba's classic American cars from the 1950s are still driven daily, kept running for decades by mechanics who can't legally import new parts.
CUW: Curacao gave its name to the blue liqueur, originally made from the dried peels of the bitter Laraha orange that grows almost nowhere else.
CYM: The Cayman Islands have more registered companies than people, thanks to their status as one of the world's largest offshore financial centers.
CYP: Cyprus has been divided since 1974 by a UN buffer zone, and its capital, Nicosia, remains the world's last divided capital city.
CZE: The Czech Republic has the highest per-capita beer consumption in the world, brewing traditions that trace back centuries to towns like Pilsen.
DEU: Germany is home to more than 1,500 registered types of sausage, and UNESCO recognized German bread culture — with over 3,000 documented recipes — as Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2014.
DJI: Djibouti hosts military bases from the U.S., France, China, and Japan simultaneously, all within a country smaller than Massachusetts.
DMA: Dominica, nicknamed the 'Nature Island,' is popularly said to have 365 rivers — one for every day of the year — though the exact number depends on how small a stream has to be to count.
DNK: Denmark's Lego bricks are precise enough that a brick made in 1958 still fits perfectly with one made today.
DOM: The Dominican Republic and Haiti share the island of Hispaniola, the only island in the world divided between two independent nations for centuries.
DZA: Algeria is Africa's largest country by land area, and over 80% of it is covered by the Sahara Desert.
ECU: Because of the equatorial bulge, the summit of Ecuador's Mount Chimborazo is the point on Earth's surface farthest from the planet's center — farther out than Everest's peak.
EGY: The Great Pyramid of Giza was the tallest human-made structure on Earth for almost 3,800 years — the longest such reign of any building in history.
ERI: Asmara, Eritrea's capital, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site nicknamed 'Africa's Art Deco city' for its remarkably preserved 1930s modernist architecture.
ESH: Western Sahara is Africa's last major unresolved colonial-era territorial dispute, claimed by both Morocco and the Sahrawi independence movement.
ESP: Spain is the only country with land on both mainland Europe and mainland Africa, via its cities of Ceuta and Melilla.
EST: Estonia was the first country to allow nationwide online voting and remains one of the most digitally governed societies on Earth.
ETH: Ethiopia runs on its own calendar, currently several years behind the Gregorian calendar, and marks the new year in September.
FIN: Finland has more saunas than cars, with an estimated one sauna for roughly every two people.
FJI: Fiji is made up of over 330 islands, but only about a third of them are permanently inhabited.
FLK: The Falkland Islands are home to roughly 500,000 sheep, outnumbering the human population by more than 130 to 1.
FRA: France is the most visited country in the world, welcoming more international tourists annually than any other nation.
FRO: The Faroe Islands have more sheep than people, and their name literally translates to 'Sheep Islands.'
FSM: The Federated States of Micronesia is spread across more than 600 islands over an expanse of Pacific roughly the size of the continental United States.
GAB: Gabon set aside roughly 11% of its land as national parkland in a single stroke in 2002, one of the highest shares of protected rainforest in the world.
GBR: 'Big Ben' technically refers only to the bell inside the clock tower, which is officially named Elizabeth Tower.
GEO: Georgia claims to be the birthplace of wine, with archaeological evidence of winemaking dating back roughly 8,000 years.
GGY: Guernsey issues its own banknotes and coins, even though it uses the British pound and isn't part of the United Kingdom or the EU.
GHA: Ghana was the first sub-Saharan African country to gain independence from colonial rule, in 1957.
GIN: Guinea holds some of the world's largest known reserves of bauxite, the ore used to produce aluminum.
GMB: The Gambia is mainland Africa's smallest country, a narrow strip of land following the Gambia River and almost entirely surrounded by Senegal.
GNB: Guinea-Bissau's Bijagos Archipelago is one of the few places in the world where hippos are known to swim in the open ocean between islands.
GNQ: Equatorial Guinea is the only African country where Spanish is an official language, a legacy of Spanish colonial rule.
GRC: In Greece, discovering ancient ruins during construction is common enough that a metro line, apartment building, or airport project can be delayed for years while archaeologists excavate the site first.
GRD: Grenada is nicknamed the 'Island of Spice' for being one of the world's largest producers of nutmeg, which even appears on its national flag.
GRL: Greenland is the world's largest island, but over 80% of it is permanently covered by an ice sheet.
GTM: Guatemala is home to over 30 volcanoes, several still active, more than any other Central American country.
GUM: Guam is often called 'where America's day begins,' since it sits just west of the international date line among the first U.S. territories to greet each new day.
GUY: Guyana is the only English-speaking country in South America, a legacy of British colonial rule as British Guiana.
HKG: Hong Kong has more skyscrapers than any other city in the world, packing thousands of tall buildings into a famously mountainous, land-scarce territory.
HMD: Heard Island is home to Australia's only active volcano, Big Ben, on a territory so remote it's rarely visited more than once a decade.
HND: Honduras is home to the second-largest barrier reef system in the world, part of the Mesoamerican Reef along its Caribbean coast.
HRV: The necktie traces back to the scarves worn by Croatian mercenaries in 17th-century France, whose name gave us the word 'cravat.'
HTI: Haiti became the first country in the world founded by a successful slave revolt, declaring independence from France in 1804.
HUN: The Rubik's Cube was invented in 1974 by Budapest architecture professor Erno Rubik, originally as a teaching tool for spatial reasoning.
IDN: Indonesia is the largest archipelago nation on Earth — its government officially registered 16,056 islands with the United Nations in 2017.
IMN: The Isle of Man's parliament, Tynwald, has met continuously since 979 AD, making it one of the oldest continuous parliaments in the world.
IND: India has more than 20 official languages and hundreds more spoken regionally, yet no single language is designated 'national' in its constitution.
INDIAN-OCEAN-TER: France's Scattered Islands in the Indian Ocean host no permanent civilian population, only rotating military and scientific personnel.
IOT: The British Indian Ocean Territory's Diego Garcia atoll hosts a major joint UK-US military base and has no permanent civilian residents.
IRL: Ireland is nicknamed the 'Emerald Isle' for its intensely green landscape, a product of near-constant Atlantic rainfall.
IRN: Iran is home to one of the oldest continuous civilizations on Earth, with the Persian Empire tracing back over 2,500 years.
IRQ: Iraq sits on the land historically known as Mesopotamia, widely considered the cradle of writing, the wheel, and humanity's earliest cities.
ISL: Iceland has no standing army and no mosquitoes, one of the only places on Earth confirmed to be mosquito-free.
ISR: The Dead Sea, bordering Israel, sits at roughly 430 meters below sea level, the lowest land elevation on Earth.
ITA: Italy has more UNESCO World Heritage Sites than any other country in the world.
JAM: Jamaica was the birthplace of reggae music and produced two of the fastest sprint times ever recorded, both by Usain Bolt.
JEY: Jersey's tidal range is among the largest in the world, with the sea receding far enough at low tide to walk to neighboring islets.
JOR: Jordan's ancient city of Petra was carved directly into rose-colored sandstone cliffs and remained largely unknown to the Western world until 1812.
JPN: Japan's Geospatial Information Authority recounted the nation's islands using modern digital mapping in 2023 and found 14,125 of them — more than double the previously cited figure of 6,800.
KAZ: Kazakhstan is the world's largest landlocked country by land area, roughly the size of Western Europe.
KEN: Kenya's Great Rift Valley is part of a tectonic split so large it's slowly pulling East Africa apart, potentially forming a new ocean over millions of years.
KGZ: Kyrgyzstan is landlocked and more than 90% mountainous, one of the most vertical countries on Earth.
KHM: Cambodia's Angkor Wat is the largest religious monument in the world by land area, originally built as a Hindu temple before becoming Buddhist.
KIR: Kiribati is the only country in the world situated in all four hemispheres — north, south, east, and west.
KNA: Saint Kitts and Nevis is the smallest sovereign country in the Americas, both by area and by population.
KOR: South Korea has one of the fastest average internet speeds in the world, a legacy of aggressive early broadband investment in the late 1990s.
KOSOVO: Kosovo declared independence from Serbia in 2008 and is recognized by roughly half of UN member states, making its sovereignty one of the most contested in the world.
KWT: Kuwait has one of the highest concentrations of proven oil reserves in the world relative to its small land area.
LAO: Laos is the only landlocked country in Southeast Asia, and parts of it remain littered with unexploded ordnance from the Vietnam War era.
LBN: Lebanon is one of the only countries where you can ski in the mountains and swim in the Mediterranean on the same day.
LBR: Liberia was founded in the 19th century by freed American slaves and modeled its flag and constitution closely on those of the United States.
LBY: Libya holds Africa's largest proven oil reserves, despite the Sahara Desert covering roughly 90% of its territory.
LCA: Saint Lucia is the only country in the world named after a woman, Saint Lucy of Syracuse.
LIE: Liechtenstein is one of only two doubly landlocked countries in the world, meaning it's surrounded entirely by other landlocked countries.
LKA: Sri Lanka was the first country in the world to elect a female head of government, Sirimavo Bandaranaike, in 1960.
LSO: Lesotho is the only country in the world located entirely above 1,000 meters in elevation, earning it the nickname 'Kingdom in the Sky.'
LTU: Lithuania was once the largest country in Europe, when its Grand Duchy stretched from the Baltic Sea nearly to the Black Sea in the 15th century.
LUX: Luxembourg has one of the highest GDP-per-capita figures in the world, and public transport across the entire country has been free since 2020.
LVA: Riga, Latvia's capital, has one of the largest and best-preserved collections of Art Nouveau buildings in Europe.
MAC: Macao generates more gambling revenue than Las Vegas, making it the largest gambling market in the world.
MAF: The island of Saint Martin is split between France and the Netherlands, one of the smallest landmasses in the world shared by two sovereign nations.
MAR: Almost none of the classic film 'Casablanca' was actually filmed in Morocco's real city of Casablanca.
MCO: Monaco is the second-smallest country in the world, yet it's more densely populated than any other sovereign nation.
MDA: Moldova's Milestii Mici wine cellar holds the largest wine collection in the world by bottle count, stretching over 200 km of underground tunnels.
MDG: Madagascar split from mainland Africa roughly 150 million years ago, leaving over 90% of its wildlife found nowhere else on Earth.
MDV: The Maldives is the lowest-lying country in the world, with an average ground elevation of just 1.5 meters above sea level.
MEX: Chocolate, corn, tomatoes, and chili peppers all spread to the rest of the world from Mexico following European contact.
MHL: The Marshall Islands' Bikini Atoll was the site of 23 nuclear tests between 1946 and 1958, and remains uninhabited by its original islanders to this day.
MKD: North Macedonia changed its name from simply 'Macedonia' in 2019 to resolve a decades-long naming dispute with neighboring Greece.
MLI: Mali's ancient city of Timbuktu was once one of the wealthiest trading centers in the world, famed for gold, salt, and a legendary library of manuscripts.
MLT: Malta's capital, Valletta, is so densely packed with historic buildings that the entire city itself is listed as a single UNESCO World Heritage Site.
MMR: Myanmar was known as Burma until a military government officially renamed it in 1989, a change still not recognized by every country.
MNE: Montenegro's name literally means 'Black Mountain,' referencing the dark, forested peaks that dominate its coastline.
MNG: Mongolia is the least densely populated sovereign country on Earth, and roughly a third of its population still lives a nomadic or semi-nomadic herding lifestyle.
MNP: The Mariana Trench, near the Northern Mariana Islands, holds the deepest known point in all of Earth's oceans, the Challenger Deep.
MOZ: Mozambique's coastline stretches for roughly 2,500 km along the Indian Ocean, among the longest of any African country.
MRT: Mauritania was the last country in the world to formally criminalize slavery, doing so in law only in 1981 and adding criminal penalties in 2007.
MSR: Montserrat's capital, Plymouth, was buried by volcanic ash after the Soufriere Hills eruptions in the 1990s and remains an abandoned exclusion zone today.
MUS: Mauritius was the only known home of the dodo bird, which went extinct within roughly a century of the island's discovery by sailors.
MWI: Lake Malawi holds more fish species than any other lake in the world, with estimates exceeding 1,000 distinct species.
MYS: Malaysia's Petronas Towers were the tallest buildings in the world when completed in 1998, and remain the tallest twin towers on Earth.
N-CYPRUS: Northern Cyprus declared independence in 1983 and is recognized by only one UN member state, Turkiye, making it one of the most diplomatically isolated territories on Earth.
NAM: Namibia was the first country in the world to write environmental protection directly into its constitution, in 1990.
NCL: New Caledonia's lagoon is one of the largest in the world and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, home to an extraordinary diversity of marine life.
NER: Niger is named for the Niger River, but roughly 80% of the country itself lies within the Sahara Desert.
NFK: Norfolk Island was originally settled to grow flax and pine for the British Navy, then later used as a particularly remote penal colony.
NGA: Nigeria's Nollywood has for years ranked among the two or three largest film industries in the world by number of films produced annually, often surpassing Hollywood in sheer volume.
NIC: Nicaragua's Lake Nicaragua is one of the only freshwater lakes in the world known to contain bull sharks, which adapted to survive without salt water.
NIU: Niue is one of the world's largest coral islands and has a population small enough that nearly everyone knows each other by name.
NLD: About a quarter of the Netherlands sits below sea level, protected by an extensive system of dikes, dunes, and pumps.
NOR: Norway's coastline, including all its fjords and islands, stretches over 100,000 km — long enough to circle the Earth roughly two and a half times.
NPL: Nepal is the only country in the world whose national flag isn't rectangular — it's made of two stacked triangular pennants.
NRU: Nauru is the smallest island nation in the world and, for a period in the 1980s, had one of the highest per-capita incomes on Earth from phosphate mining.
NZL: New Zealand was the first country in the world to grant women the right to vote in parliamentary elections, in 1893.
OMN: Oman's frankincense trade helped make the region one of the ancient world's most valuable trade routes, with the resin once worth its weight in gold.
PAK: Pakistan is home to K2, the second-highest mountain on Earth, and more peaks over 8,000 meters than any country besides Nepal and China.
PAN: The Panama Canal is one of the only places in the world where ships heading from the Atlantic to the Pacific effectively travel from northwest to southeast.
PCN: The Pitcairn Islands have one of the smallest populations of any territory in the world, descended largely from the mutineers of HMS Bounty and their Tahitian companions.
PER: Peru's Machu Picchu sits at over 2,400 meters and was unknown to the wider world until its 1911 rediscovery by American historian Hiram Bingham.
PHL: The Philippines is made up of 7,641 islands, officially counted by the national mapping agency (NAMRIA) in 2016 — many of the smallest are still uninhabited and unnamed.
PLW: Palau's Jellyfish Lake is home to millions of jellyfish that have lost their sting over thousands of years of evolving without natural predators.
PNG: Papua New Guinea is one of the most linguistically diverse places on Earth, with over 800 distinct languages spoken.
POL: Poland's Wieliczka Salt Mine has been continuously mined since the 13th century and contains entire chapels carved out of rock salt.
PRI: Puerto Rico was once home to the Arecibo Observatory, for decades one of the largest radio telescopes ever built and used in the search for extraterrestrial signals.
PRK: North Korea and South Korea remain technically at war, since the 1953 Korean War ended with an armistice, not a formal peace treaty.
PRT: Portugal's Age of Exploration in the 15th century helped launch the first truly global trade networks, connecting Europe, Africa, Asia, and the Americas.
PRY: Paraguay generates nearly all of its domestic electricity from hydropower and is a net exporter of electricity thanks to the massive Itaipu Dam.
PSE: The State of Palestine is recognized by a majority of United Nations member states, though it holds only non-member observer status at the UN itself.
PYF: French Polynesia's Bora Bora is credited as the birthplace of the overwater bungalow, a hospitality concept believed to have originated there in the 1960s.
QAT: Qatar has one of the highest GDP-per-capita figures in the world, built largely on the country's vast natural gas reserves.
ROU: Romania's Sighișoara, in Transylvania, is the birthplace of Vlad the Impaler — the 15th-century Wallachian ruler whose brutal reputation inspired Dracula.
RUS: Russia spans eleven time zones, more than any other country in the world.
RWA: Rwanda banned single-use plastic bags nationwide in 2008, well before most of the world began considering similar policies.
SAU: Saudi Arabia has no permanent rivers anywhere in the country, one of the largest nations on Earth without a single one.
SDN: Sudan has more pyramids than Egypt, with over 200 ancient Nubian pyramids scattered across its desert.
SEN: Senegal's Lake Retba, also called Lac Rose, turns a distinctive pink color due to algae that thrive in its extremely salty water.
SGP: Singapore is one of only three surviving city-states in the world, alongside Monaco and Vatican City.
SGS: South Georgia has no permanent civilian population but hosts more breeding king penguins than almost anywhere else on Earth.
SHN: Saint Helena is one of the most remote inhabited islands in the world and was the site of Napoleon Bonaparte's final exile until his death in 1821.
SIACHEN-GLACIER: The Siachen Glacier, disputed between India and Pakistan, is the highest battlefield on Earth, with troops stationed at altitudes above 6,000 meters.
SLB: The Solomon Islands were the site of some of the fiercest Pacific battles of World War II, including the pivotal Battle of Guadalcanal.
SLE: Sierra Leone's capital, Freetown, was founded in 1792 as a settlement for freed and formerly enslaved people, giving the city its name.
SLV: El Salvador became the first country in the world to adopt Bitcoin as legal tender, in 2021.
SMR: San Marino claims to be the oldest surviving sovereign state and constitutional republic in the world, founded, by tradition, in the year 301 AD.
SOM: Somalia has the longest coastline of any country on mainland Africa.
SOMALILAND: Somaliland declared independence from Somalia in 1991 and functions as a de facto independent state, though no UN member country formally recognizes it.
SPM: Saint Pierre and Miquelon is the last remaining piece of the once-vast territory of New France still governed by France, just off the coast of Canada.
SRB: Belgrade has reportedly been destroyed and rebuilt around 40 times throughout its history, owing to its position at the confluence of the Danube and Sava rivers — one of the most fought-over city sites in Europe.
SSD: South Sudan became the world's newest country in 2011, seceding from Sudan after decades of civil war.
STP: Sao Tome and Principe is Africa's second-smallest country, and its economy was historically built almost entirely on cocoa production.
SUR: Suriname is the smallest sovereign country in South America, and over 90% of its territory remains covered in pristine rainforest.
SVK: Slovakia is home to over 100 castles and around 400 chateaux and manor houses — one of the highest concentrations of historic fortifications in Europe, packed into a country roughly the size of Vermont and New Hampshire combined.
SVN: Slovenia's capital, Ljubljana, was European Green Capital in 2016, with more than half the city's area given over to green space.
SWE: Sweden's legal tradition of Allemansratten, the 'right to roam,' lets anyone walk, camp, or forage on almost any land, public or private.
SWZ: Eswatini was known as Swaziland until 2018, when King Mswati III officially renamed the country to mark 50 years of independence.
SXM: Sint Maarten is famous for Maho Beach, where jets land so low overhead that tourists gather just to feel the engine blast.
SYC: Seychelles is home to the coco de mer, a palm tree that produces the largest and heaviest seed of any plant on Earth.
SYR: Syria's capital, Damascus, is considered one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world, with habitation dating back thousands of years.
TCA: The Turks and Caicos Islands are home to one of the largest barrier reef systems in the world, drawing divers from across the globe.
TCD: Lake Chad has shrunk by roughly 90% since the 1960s, once one of the largest lakes in Africa and now a fraction of its former size.
TGO: Togo is one of the narrowest countries in Africa, stretching just over 50 km wide at its narrowest point.
THA: Thailand is the only country in Southeast Asia never colonized by a European power.
TJK: Tajikistan is one of the most mountainous countries in the world, with over 90% of its territory covered by mountains.
TKM: Turkmenistan's Darvaza gas crater, nicknamed the 'Door to Hell,' has been burning in the Karakum Desert for decades — locals still debate exactly when and how it was first ignited.
TLS: Timor-Leste became Asia's newest country in 2002, after gaining full independence following decades of Indonesian and earlier Portuguese rule.
TON: Tonga is the only Pacific island nation never formally colonized by a foreign power. Its monarchy traces back over a thousand years through several ruling dynasties, though the current House of Tupou has reigned since only 1875.
TTO: Trinidad and Tobago is the birthplace of steelpan music, invented from repurposed oil drums in the 20th century.
TUN: Tunisia's desert town of Matmata, with its underground cave dwellings, was used as a filming location for Luke Skywalker's home on Tatooine in Star Wars.
TUR: Turkiye's largest city, Istanbul, straddles two continents, split by the Bosphorus Strait between Europe and Asia.
TWN: Taiwan produces the vast majority of the world's most advanced computer chips, largely through a single company, TSMC.
TZA: Tanzania is home to both the highest point in Africa, Mount Kilimanjaro, and one of the continent's lowest points, the floor of Lake Tanganyika.
UGA: Uganda is home to more than half of the world's remaining mountain gorillas, primarily in its Bwindi Impenetrable Forest.
UKR: Ukraine's extraordinarily fertile black soil, called chernozem, made it historically known as the 'breadbasket of Europe.'
URY: Uruguay was the first country in the world to fully legalize the production, sale, and use of recreational cannabis nationwide, in 2013.
USA: The United States has no official national language at the federal level, despite English being the de facto language of government and commerce.
UZB: Uzbekistan is one of only two doubly landlocked countries in the world, meaning every country that borders it is also landlocked.
VAT: Vatican City is the smallest sovereign state in the world, with a population under 1,000 and its own train station, postal system, and currency.
VCT: Saint Vincent and the Grenadines' Wallilabou Bay was a filming location for Pirates of the Caribbean, and its film-set dock structures still stand today.
VEN: Venezuela's Angel Falls is the tallest uninterrupted waterfall in the world, dropping nearly 980 meters from the top of a flat-topped mountain called a tepui.
VGB: The British Virgin Islands has more registered offshore companies than people, a legacy of its role as a major international financial center.
VIR: The U.S. Virgin Islands drive on the left side of the road, despite being U.S. territory where nearly everywhere else drives on the right.
VNM: Vietnam is the world's second-largest coffee exporter, behind only Brazil, thanks to French colonial-era plantations that took root in its highlands.
VUT: Vanuatu's Mount Yasur is one of the most accessible active volcanoes on Earth, letting visitors stand near its erupting crater rim.
WLF: Wallis and Futuna is one of the least-visited territories on Earth, lacking an airport capable of handling large international flights until relatively recently.
WSM: Samoa skipped December 30, 2011 entirely, jumping forward a full day to switch sides of the International Date Line and align with its main trading partners.
YEM: Yemen's Socotra archipelago is home to the alien-looking dragon's blood tree, found nowhere else on Earth and named for its deep red sap.
ZAF: South Africa has three capital cities — Pretoria, Cape Town, and Bloemfontein — each hosting a different branch of government.
ZMB: Zambia's Victoria Falls, shared with Zimbabwe, is classified as the largest sheet of falling water in the world by combined height and width.
ZWE: Zimbabwe takes its name from Great Zimbabwe, a medieval stone city whose ruins are the largest ancient stone structure in sub-Saharan Africa — built entirely without mortar.
